coredata.dc:title: Boost the performance of inverted perovskite solar cells with PEDOT:PSS/Graphene quantum dots composite hole transporting layer
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Weiwei
Cheng, Nian
Cao, Yang
Zhao, Zhiqiang
Xiao, Zhenyu
Zi, Wei
Sun, Zhuzhu
coredata.dc:description: The inverted p-i-n structure perovskite solar cells show great potential in wearable devices and flexible solar cells. Yet the commonly used PEDOT:PSS hole transporting layer still needs to be optimized in order to pursue superior performances. Here, we have demonstrated that PEDOT:PSS/GQDs composite film can be a potential alternative to replace the PEDOT:PSS. Under optimized conditions, average power conversion efficiencies are boosted from 12.77% for perovskite solar cells fabricated on PEDOT:PSS film to 15.24% for perovskite solar cells fabricated on PEDOT:PSS/GQDs film. A champion power conversion efficiency of 16.15% has also been demonstrated with the optimized PEDOT:PSS/GQDs composite film.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Inverted perovskite solar cell
PEDOT:PSS
Graphene quantum dots
Composite hole transporting layer
Charge collection efficiency

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of the perovskite solar cells

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work receives financial support from Doctoral Scientific Research Foundation of Xinyang Normal University, Nanhu Scholars Program for Young Scholars of XYNU, and Key Project of Henan Province Colleges and Universities Plan (No. 19A430022).

This work receives financial support from Doctoral Scientific Research Foundation of Xinyang Normal University, Nanhu Scholars Program for Young Scholars of XYNU , and Key Project of Henan Province Colleges and Universities Plan (No. 19A430022 ). Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Variations of open circuit voltage (Voc), short circuit current density (Jsc), fill factor (FF) and power conversion efficiency (PCE) with different PEDOT:PSS/GQDs films.

(a) Typical J-V curves, (b) Statistical PCE distributions, (c) IPCE spectra, and (d) Continuous output current density measurements for perovskite solar cells with GQD0 and GQD2 hole transporting layer respectively.
(a) Champion J-V curves and (b) Long-term stability of perovskite solar cells fabricated on GQD0 and GQD2 films. The stability test is conducted in ambient atmosphere with humidity around 20–40%.

Planar SEM images of perovskite films deposited on (a) GQD0, (b) GQD1, (c) GQD2, (d) GQD3, and (e) GQD4 films, respectively. Two representative defects in the perovskite crystals are marked out using white circles.

(a) XRD patterns of perovskite films deposited on PEDOT:PSS/GQDs films, (b) Magnified XRD patterns of perovskite films showing the presence of PbI2 diffraction peaks. Asterisk (*) indicates the characteristic diffraction peaks of MAPbI3 perovskites.
(a) Schematic device structure for measuring the current-voltage curves, (b) Corresponding current-voltage curves of GQDn (n = 0, 1, 2, 3, and 4) films.

(a) Dark current density-voltage curves, (b) Normalized photocurrent density-effective voltage (Jph-Veff) curves on a double-logarithmic scale for perovskite solar cells fabricated on GQD0 and GQD2 films. The photocurrent densities are normalized to the saturation photocurrent density (Jsat) at Veff = 1.2 V. The saturation photocurrent densities are 20.42 mA cm−2 and 21.40 mA cm−2 for perovskite solar cells fabricated on GQD0 and GQD2 films respectively. (c) Steady-state photoluminescence spectra and (d) Time-resolved photoluminescence spectra for perovskite films deposited on FTO/GQD0 and FTO/GQD2 films.

head.ce:title: Boost the performance of inverted perovskite solar cells with PEDOT:PSS/Graphene quantum dots composite hole transporting layer

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Physics and Electronic Engineering, Xinyang Normal University, 237 Nanhu Road, Xinyang, 464000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Physics and Electronic Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Physics and Electronic Engineering, Xinyang Normal University, 237 Nanhu Road, Xinyang, 464000, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Energy-Saving Building Materials Collaborative Innovation Center of Henan Province, Xinyang Normal University, 237 Nanhu Road, Xinyang, 464000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Energy-Saving Building Materials Collaborative Innovation Center of Henan Province

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Energy-Saving Building Materials Collaborative Innovation Center of Henan Province, Xinyang Normal University, 237 Nanhu Road, Xinyang, 464000, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The inverted p-i-n structure perovskite solar cells show great potential in wearable devices and flexible solar cells. Yet the commonly used PEDOT:PSS hole transporting layer still needs to be optimized in order to pursue superior performances. Here, we have demonstrated that PEDOT:PSS/GQDs composite film can be a potential alternative to replace the PEDOT:PSS. Under optimized conditions, average power conversion efficiencies are boosted from 12.77% for perovskite solar cells fabricated on PEDOT:PSS film to 15.24% for perovskite solar cells fabricated on PEDOT:PSS/GQDs film. A champion power conversion efficiency of 16.15% has also been demonstrated with the optimized PEDOT:PSS/GQDs composite film.

ce:section.ce:para: Perovskite solar cells have become the hotspot in the field of thin film solar cells due to its high power conversion efficiencies and low fabrication cost. Power conversion efficiencies have been promoted from 3.8% in 2009 to 25.2% in 2019 during a period of about 10 years [1], this amazing growth rate in power conversion efficiencies is unprecedented compared with the other type of solar cells. Perovskite solar cells can be roughly divided into conventional n-i-p structure and the inverted p-i-n structure. The conventional structure has demonstrated superior power conversion efficiencies [2–5], however, the commonly used TiO2 electron transporting layer needs high temperature annealing and not suitable for flexible solar cells. On the contrary, the inverted structure solar cells exhibit power conversion efficiencies slightly lower than the conventional structure [6,7], but they can be fabricated in a low temperature process, thus are very promising in wearable devices and flexible solar cells.

In the inverted perovskite solar cells, PEDOT:PSS is the most commonly used hole transporting layer due to its high transparency, moderate conductivity, and feasible low temperature solution process [8–10]. Yet, the highest power conversion efficiencies of perovskite solar cells fabricated on PEDOT:PSS are reported to be around 19% [7,11], which are lower than most high performance perovskite solar cells. The work function of pristine PEDOT:PSS is much lower than the valence band edge of perovskite light absorbing layer, this mismatch of band alignment results in energy losses during the hole extraction process and a low Voc less than 1.0 V is usually observed [8,12]. At the same time, conductivity of PEDOT:PSS is lower than that of the widely used [6,6]-phenyl-C61-butyric acid methyl ester (PC61BM) electron transporting layer. The unbalanced charge transportation in the hole transporting layer and the electron transporting layer can create charge carrier accumulation, leading to large leakage current and low fill factor [13,14].

To solve the above mentioned problems, various methods have been proposed, such as solvent treatment, employment of an additive, and formation of PEDOT:PSS/nanomaterial composite hole transporting layer [8,10,15–18]. All these methods can enhance the performances of inverted perovskite solar cells fabricated on PEDOT:PSS films due to one or more of the following factors: (1) modifying the morphology of the resulting PEDOT:PSS film, and gradually leading to perovskite films with higher crystallinity and less traps or defects, (2) adjusting the work function of PEDOT:PSS film to better match that of the perovskite film, and leading to an increase of the open circuit voltage, (3) increasing the conductivity of the PEDOT:PSS film, and facilitating hole extraction and transportation.

Composite hole transporting layers containing PEDOT:PSS and an extra nanomaterial are widely studied as potential candidate to replace the pristine PEDOT:PSS film in order to pursue superior performances. Metal nanoparticles, CrO3, CuSCN, V2O5, WO3 et al. have been employed to form composite hole transporting films together with PEDOT:PSS [19–23]. Besides the above mentioned materials, carbon materials, such as carbon black [24], carbon nanotubes [16], carbon dot [17], graphene oxide [18] et al. have also been successfully explored to form composite hole transporting film with PEDOT:PSS. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are single, double and few layered graphene sheets with dimensions less than 100 nm [25–28]. Usually, GQDs are functionalized with hydroxyl and carboxylic acid groups, and a schematic structure of single layered GQDs is illustrated in supporting information Fig. S1. Due to its high conductivity, excellent solubility, and stable photoluminescence, GQDs have gained widespread interests. GQDs have been studied to serve as hole transporting layer in organic solar cells [29], yielding power conversion efficiencies comparable to that of PEDOT:PSS. Later, PEDOT:PSS films incorporating GQDs are demonstrated to be superior hole transporting layer both in organic solar cells and Si/PEDOT:PSS hybrid solar cells [30,31]. GQDs are believed to strongly interact with the PEDOT and PSS chain, leading to a phase separation between PEDOT and PSS, thus conductivity of the PEDOT:PSS/GQDs composite film is enhanced [32]. The resulting better hole transporting capability of the PEDOT:PSS/GQDs composite film is the main reason for the enhancement of the performances of the above mentioned solar cells.

In this report, PEDOT:PSS/GQDs composite films are prepared by spin coating a mixture of commercial PEDOT:PSS solution and GQDs solution, the performances of the inverted perovskite solar cells with different GQDs concentrations are studied. The optimized PEDOT:PSS/GQDs composite film can boost the average power conversion efficiency to 15.24%, which is enhanced by 19% compared with the PEDOT:PSS film. A champion power conversion efficiency of 16.16% is also obtained with the optimized PEDOT:PSS/GQDs composite film.

GQDs aqueous solution (1 mg mL−1) was purchased from Tanfeng Graphene Technology (Suzhou) Co. Ltd. The average size of the GQDs is about 15 nm, the thicknesses of the GQDs are between 0.5 nm and 2.0 nm according to the product specification. Clevios PVP AI 4083 PEDOT:PSS was bought from Heraeus (Germany). PbI2, CH3NH3I (MAI) and PC61BM were purchased from Xi'an Polymer Light Technology Corp. To prepare the PEDOT:PSS/GQDs composite films, 1 mL pristine PEDOT:PSS solution was mixed with x mL GQDs solution and (3-x) mL deionized water, the mixed solution was ultrasoniced for 30 min before use. The total volume of the PEDOT:PSS/GQDs solution is 4 mL, which can ensure that the final film thicknesses of the PEDOT:PSS/GQDs composite films are nearly the same. The values of x were 0, 0.5, 1.0, 1.5, and 2.0 respectively, corresponding PEDOT:PSS/GQDs films are referred as GQD0, GQD1, GQD2, GQD3, and GQD4 in the following context.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of the perovskite solar cells
ce:section.ce:para: FTO glasses are thoroughly cleaned using deionized water and ethanol in an ultrasonic bath. After being dried, the FTO glasses are subjected to an ultraviolet ozone treatment for 15 min. PEDOT:PSS/GQDs solutions are filtrated with a 0.45 μm filter, dynamically spin coated onto the FTO substrates at 3000 rpm for 30 s, and annealed at 150 °C for 15 min in the air. Then, the substrates are transferred into an Ar filled glove box for further deposition of perovskite and PC61BM layers. The perovskite precursor solution is prepared by mixing 461 mg PbI2 and 159 mg MAI into a mixture solution of 78 mg DMSO and 1 mL DMF, and magnetic stirred at 50 °C for one night. Perovskite solution is spin coated onto the PEDOT:PSS/GQDs film at 1000 rpm for 10 s and 4000 rpm for 30 s, after 6 s at 4000 rpm, 450 μL chlorobenzene is quickly dripped onto the wet perovskite film. The obtained perovskite films are further annealed at 100 °C for 10 min and naturally cool down to room temperature. PC61BM (20 mg mL−1 in chlorobenzene) is spin coated onto the perovskite layer at 2000 rpm for 30 s. Finally, Ag electrode is thermally evaporated onto the PC61BM layer.

XRD patterns of the perovskite films are obtained using X-ray diffraction (SmartLab 9 kW, Rigaku) with Cu Kα radiation. UV–vis transmittance and absorbance spectra of the PEDOT:PSS/GQDs and perovskite films are characterized using Lambda 950 UV–vis spectrometer. Planar view SEM images of the perovskite films are observed on Hitachi S4800. AFM images of the PEDOT:PSS/GQDs films are obtained using Dimension Icon (Bruker). Current density-voltage curves of the perovskite solar cells are tested under simulated sunlight in the ambient atmosphere (relative humidity ~40%). A XEF-300 solar simulator is used to provide the standard AM 1.5 sunlight (100 mW cm−2). A metal mask (0.1 cm2) is used to define the effective area of the solar cells. A Keithley 2450 sourcemeter is used to measure the current density-voltage properties of the solar cells. In the reverse scan mode, the voltage is scanned from 1.1 V to −0.2 V, in the forward scan mode, the voltage is scanned from −0.2 V to 1.1 V. Scan speed is set to be 20 mV s−1. Incident photo-to-electron conversion efficiency (IPCE) measurement is conducted on commercial quantum efficiency system (QE-R, Enli Technology Co., Ltd) under AC mode. For long-term stability test, current density-voltage curves of perovskite solar cells are measured in the ambient atmosphere, during the interval of test, perovskite solar cells are stored in a desiccator with humidity around 20%. Photoluminescence (exciting at 481.5 nm) and time-resolved photoluminescence (exciting at 481.5 nm and emission at 760 nm) are measured with an Edinburgh Instrument (FLS2100). The time-resolved photoluminescence curves are fitted using a bi-exponential decay function
f
(
x
)
=
A
1
exp
(
− x /
t
1
)
+
A
2
exp
( − x /
t
2
)
, the average decay time is calculated using the equation
t
a v e
=
(
A
1
t
1
2
+
A
2
t
2
2
)
/
(
A
1
t
1
+
A
2
t
2
)
[33].

GQDs are incorporated into the PEDOT:PSS films by mixing different volume of GQDs aqueous solution with 1 mL commercial PEDOT:PSS solution. To ensure that the final film thicknesses of the PEDOT:PSS/GQDs films are nearly the same, deionized water is added and the final volumes of the mixed PEDOT:PSS/GQDs solutions are the same. The PEDOT:PSS/GQDs films are prepared using a simple spin coating process. Perovskite solar cells with a p-i-n structure are fabricated on these PEDOT:PSS/GQDs films and the corresponding current density-voltage (J-V) characteristics are measured under simulated AM 1.5 sunlight. The open circuit voltage (Voc), short circuit current density (Jsc), fill factor (FF) and power conversion efficiency (PCE) from a batch of 10 solar cells are extracted from the J-V curves and presented in Fig. 1 . Perovskite solar cells employing GQD0 hole transporting layer exhibit an average Voc of 978 mV, Jsc of 18.31 mA cm−2, FF of 68.01%, and PCE of 12.18%. The obtained Voc is much larger than most reported Vocs of perovskite solar cells using pristine PEDOT:PSS hole transporting layer, and is comparable with the reported Voc in Ref. [34]. The work function of the PEDOT:PSS film is enhanced when the pristine PEDOT:PSS solution is diluted with water [34], leading to a better energy level match with the perovskite film and larger Voc in the corresponding perovskite solar cells. When GQDs are incorporated into the PEDOT:PSS films, no clear variation trend is observed for the average Voc in Fig. 1a. Average Vocs of the perovskite solar cells spread between 969 mV and 995 mV, the highest Voc is obtained with the GQD2 films. Clear variation trends are observed for the Jsc, FF, and PCE, as can be seen in Fig. 1b–d. All these values first increase with increasing volume of the GQDs solution, and then decrease after the optimum volume of GQDs solution. The optimized performances are observed for the GQD2 hole transporting layer, which can demonstrate an average Voc of 995 mV, Jsc of 21.07 mA cm−2, FF of 72.68%, and PCE of 15.24%. Compared with GQD0 films, the GQD2 films can lead to much enhanced performance, which mainly originates from higher Jsc and larger FF.

To further confirm that perovskite solar cells fabricated on GQD2 films exhibit superior performance compared with solar cells with GQD0 films, a batch of 28 solar cells are fabricated on both the GQD0 and GQD2 films. Typical J-V curves of perovskite solar cells are presented in Fig. 2 a. In the reverse scan mode, solar cells fabricated on GQD0 film exhibits Voc of 983 mV, Jsc of 18.73 mA cm−2, FF of 69.40%, and PCE of 12.77%. In the forward scan mode, the corresponding Voc, Jsc, FF, and PCE are 983 mV, 18.65 mA cm−2, 69.51%, and 12.74%, respectively. In comparison, solar cells fabricated on GQD2 film exhibits Voc of 1002 mV, Jsc of 20.77 mA cm−2, FF of 72.96%, PCE of 15.18% in the reverse scan, and Voc of 996 mV, Jsc of 20.61 mA cm−2, FF of 74.23%, and PCE of 15.24% in the forward scan. The measured J-V curves coincide with each other both in the reverse and forward scan mode, and demonstrate negligible hysteresis. Statistical PCEs of the solar cells are summarized in Fig. 2b. The average PCE of GQD0 based perovskite solar cells is 12.77 ± 0.29%, compared with GQD0 based solar cells, average PCE of GQD2 based perovskite solar cells is 15.24 ± 0.46%, which is enhanced by 19%. Therefore, it is quite obvious that GQD2 film can lead to better PCEs. IPCE spectra of the perovskite solar cells are presented in Fig. 2c. The IPCE values of perovskite solar cell fabricated on GQD2 film are larger than that of solar cells with GQD0 film in the wavelength range from 350 nm to 760 nm, which is consistent with the observed higher Jsc in the J-V curves. Continuous output current densities of the perovskite solar cells at maximum power point are measured and the results are given in Fig. 2d. Solar cells fabricated on GQD0 and GQD2 films are biased at 800 mV, the output current densities are monitored for a period of 300 s. The output current densities of solar cells fabricated on GQD0 and GQD2 films are 16.24 mA cm−2 and 19.16 mA cm−2, corresponding to a PCE of 13.0% and 15.33% respectively. The measured output current densities are nearly constant during the whole period, indicating excellent device stability under light illumination.

Fig. 3 a presents the champion J-V curves of perovskite solar cells fabricated on GQD0 and GQD2 films. Champion solar cell based on GQD0 film exhibits a Voc of 972 mV, Jsc of 18.56 mA cm−2, FF of 73.62%, and PCE of 13.28% in the reverse scan mode, and Voc of 972 mV, Jsc of 18.53 mA cm−2, FF of 72.98%, PCE of 13.14% in the forward scan mode. At the same time, champion solar cell based on GQD2 film exhibits Voc of 997 mV, Jsc of 21.43 mA cm−2, FF of 73.53%, PCE of 15.71% in the reverse scan mode, and Voc of 1002 mV, Jsc of 21.41 mA cm−2, FF of 75.31%, PCE of 16.16% in the forward scan mode. Long-term stability test of the perovskite solar cells is conducted in ambient atmosphere and the corresponding results are presented in Fig. 3b. The PCEs of both kind of perovskite solar cells slowly degrade with extending storage time. After 9 days, perovskite solar cell fabricated on GQD0 film only preserves 58% of its initial PCE value. In contrast, perovskite solar cell fabricated on GQD2 film preserves 77% of its initial value. Therefore, perovskite solar cells with GQD2 hole transporting layer demonstrates superior long-term stability.

Transmittance spectra of the PEDOT:PSS/GQDs films are presented in Fig. S2. All the PEDOT:PSS/GQDs films exhibit comparable transmittance compared with bare FTO substrate due to the very thin film thicknesses of the PEDOT:PSS/GQDs films. UV–vis absorbance spectra of the perovskite films deposited on PEDOT:PSS/GQDs films are shown in Fig. S3, all the perovskite films show nearly the same absorbance. The representative AFM images of the PEDOT:PSS/GQDs films are shown in Fig. S4. The root-mean-squared (RMS) roughness of bare FTO substrate is 30 nm, when a thin PEDOT:PSS/GQDs film is deposited onto the FTO substrate, the RMS roughness decreases to around 24 nm. At the same, all the PEDOT:PSS/GQDs films exhibit nearly the same RMS roughness. Fig. 4 presents the planar SEM images of perovskite films deposited on PEDOT:PSS/GQDs films. All the perovskite films exhibit the same morphology due to the same RMS roughness of the PEDOT:PSS/GQDs films, except obvious defects are observed in the GQD4 film. As discussed in the previous context, the UV–vis absorption and SEM morphology of the perovskite films deposited on PEDOT:PSS/GQDs films are nearly the same, which cannot explain the observed performance enhancement in the corresponding perovskite solar cells.

Fig. 5 a presents the XRD patterns of the perovskite films. Characteristic diffraction peaks located at around 14.1°, 19.9°, 23.5°, 24.5°, 28.4°, and 31.9° are clearly observed, indicating the formation of MAPbI3 perovskite crystals [35]. Magnified XRD patterns are presented in Fig. 5b. For perovskite deposited on GQD0 (PEDOT:PSS without GQDs) film, no clear diffraction peaks corresponding to PbI2 can be detected. When GQDs are incorporated into the PEDOT:PSS films, diffraction peaks corresponding to PbI2 can be detected, and the peak intensities increase with higher GQDs concentrations. This small amount of residual PbI2 in the MAPbI3 perovskite films might be helpful to passivate the defects and result in higher performances [36,37].

Conductivity of the PEDOT:PSS/GQDs film is measured using the schematic device structure in Fig. 6 a. The current-voltage curves from the two Ag electrodes are presented in Fig. 6b. The corresponding conductivity values of all the PEDOT:PSS/GQDs films can be calculated using equation
σ =
1
R
×
L
W × d
, where σ is the film conductivity, W, L, and d are width, length, and thickness of the PEDOT:PSS/GQDs film [10]. R is the resistance, which is extracted from the inverse slope of the current-voltage curve. W and L are 1 cm and 0.1 cm, respectively. d is about 30 nm which is derived from the AFM measurement. Using the above values, the conductivity of the GQD0 film is 1.05 × 10−4 S cm−1, which is comparable to the reported values in the literatures [15,38]. When GQDs are incorporated into the PEDOT:PSS films, the interaction between the GQDs and PEDOT:PSS will result in phase segregation between the conductive PEDOT chain and the insulated PSS chain, leading to an increase in the conductivity of the PEDOT:PSS/GQDs films [32]. Therefore, conductivities of the GQD1, GQD2, GQD3, and GQD4 films are increased to 2.56 × 10−4 S cm−1, 3.05 × 10−4 S cm−1, 3.50 × 10−4 S cm−1, and 4.09 × 10−4 S cm−1, respectively. This increased conductivity of the PEDOT:PSS/GQDs film will facilitates better hole transportation across the hole transporting layer and result in larger Jsc and FF of the perovskite solar cells.

Fig. 7 a presents the dark J-V curves of the perovskite solar cells. Compared with GQD0 hole transporting layer, perovskite solar cells fabricated on GQD2 films exhibit lower leakage current, thus lower reversed saturated current density at the same time, due to better charge transporting capability of the GQD2 film. Generally, Voc of solar cells can be expressed as
V
o c
=
n k T
q
ln
(
J
s c
J
0
+ 1 )
, where n, k, T, q, Jsc and J0 are ideality factor, Boltzmann constant, absolute temperature, elementary charge, short circuit current density and reversed saturated current density, respectively. As discussed in the above context, perovskite solar cells fabricated on GQD2 films exhibit lower J0 and larger Jsc, which contributes to the slightly enhanced Voc [19]. Fig. 7b presents the normalized photocurrent density-effective voltage (Jph-Veff) curves of the perovskite solar cells [39–41]. Jph = JL-JD, where JL and JD are the photocurrent densities measured under light illumination and dark conditions, respectively. Veff = V0-Va, where Va is the applied voltage bias, V0 is the voltage bias when Jph equals to zero. The saturation photocurrent density (Jsat) are 20.42 mA cm−2 and 21.40 mA cm−2 for perovskite solar cells fabricated on GQD0 and GQD2 films respectively, which are determined at Veff of 1.2 V. The charge collection efficiency (ηcc) is defined as Jph/Jsat [39]. As can be seen in Fig. 7b, perovskite solar cells fabricated on GQD2 films exhibit larger ηcc, which indicates a better charge collection efficiency and consists with the observed higher Jsc and FF in the corresponding perovskite solar cells. Steady-state photoluminescence (PL) and time-resolved photoluminescence (TRPL) spectra are further carried out to characterize the charge extraction capabilities of the GQD0 and GQD2 films. As can be seen in Fig. 7c, a strong emission peak can be detected at around 760 nm, which originates from the radiative recombination from the MAPbI3 perovskite film. Compared with FTO substrate, PL intensities of perovskite films deposited on FTO/GQD0 and FTO/GQD2 films are substantially quenched due to hole extraction from the perovskite films by the GQD0 and GQD2 films. Perovskite film deposited on FTO/GQD2 film demonstrates slightly decreased PL intensity compared to perovskite film deposited on FTO/GQD0 film, indicating better hole extraction capability of the GQD2 film. TRPL spectra of perovskite films deposited on the FTO/GQD0 and FTO/GQD2 films are presented in Fig. 7d. The obtained curves are fitted with the bi-exponential decay functions, the average decay time for FTO/GQD0 and FTO/GQD2 films are calculated to be 5.18 ns and 2.32 ns, respectively. The shorter decay time for FTO/GQD2 films corresponds to more efficient hole transportation from perovskite film to GQD2 film, which is consistent with the Jph-Veff and steady-state PL results.

In conclusion, PEDOT:PSS/GQDs composite films are employed as hole transporting layer for inverted structure perovskite solar cells. Residual PbI2 is detected in the perovskite films fabricated on PEDOT:PSS/GQDs films, which might be helpful to passivate the defects of the perovskite films. Current-voltage curves of the PEDOT:PSS/GQDs films confirm that the conductivity of the films are increased when GQDs are incorporated into the PEDOT:PSS films. This enhanced conductivity of the PEDOT:PSS/GQDs film can facilitate better hole transportation and result in superior charge collection efficiency. As a result, perovskite solar cells fabricated on PEDOT:PSS/GQDs films can boost the average power conversion efficiencies from 12.77% for the reference cells to 15.24% for the optimized PEDOT:PSS/GQDs film.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work receives financial support from Doctoral Scientific Research Foundation of Xinyang Normal University, Nanhu Scholars Program for Young Scholars of XYNU, and Key Project of Henan Province Colleges and Universities Plan (No. 19A430022).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.orgel.2019.105575.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Best Research-Cell Efficiency Chart. https://www.nrel.gov/pv/cell-efficiency.html

sb:title.sb:maintitle: An interface stabilized perovskite solar cell with high stabilized efficiency and low voltage loss
Energy Environ. Sci.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J.J. Yoo, S. Wieghold, M.C. Sponseller, M.R. Chua, S.N. Bertram, N.T.P. Hartono, J.S. Tresback, E.C. Hansen, J.-P. Correa-Baena, V. Bulovic, T. Buonassisi, S.S.
